result:
There is a place on the map that folds back on itself, a pleat in the paper where the town called Elsewhere hides.  The crease is so fine that atlases reproduce it as a single gray thread; only children cut along it with scissors, pop the flat world into three dimensions, and step through.  Adults lose the trick of believing in scissors sharp enough to trim reality, so the town keeps its population low.

I arrived in Elsewhere at the age of twenty-nine and a half, carrying a suitcase that grew lighter every block because the clothes inside were forgetting their purpose.  By the time I reached the boardinghouse on Sometime Street, the case was empty except for a copper penny dated 1943 and a note I had not written: “Use me only after midnight.”  I checked in anyway; the landlady took the penny for deposit, bit it, nodded, and handed me a key shaped like a question mark.  The key fit no lock I could find, so I wore it as a pendant and felt slightly more answered.

The town smelled of tin roofs after rain and of bread that remembered wheat.  Clocks ran both ways; if you stood between two storefronts you could watch your own future step out of the barber shop, hair shorter, while your past ducked into the bakery, pockets fuller.  Neither version waved.  It was considered impolite to acknowledge yourself in transit, like staring at a stranger’s cast.

I found work at the Archive of Unfinished Things, a brick building that exhaled sighs every evening at closing time.  Inside were shelves of half-knitted scarves, their stitches holding months of winter that never arrived; novels lacking only last sentences; blueprints for bridges that would span the gap between two hearts but not two shores.  My job was to catalogue the almost, to weigh the missing ounces, to keep the dust from settling too comfortably on what people had given up.

The index cards were handwritten in a language that changed nouns according to regret.  A sweater abandoned in childhood became smaller each time its mother remembered the child had grown.  A love letter never mailed absorbed carbon from the air until the paper was too heavy to lift.  I learned to read by the increase in gravity.

Every noon the town square filled with residents trading lost hours.  They carried them in matchboxes, careful not to strike time against friction.  One woman offered me three afternoons from 1998 in exchange for one sleepless night; she wanted to finish a dream whose ending she’d misplaced.  I had no sleeplessness to spare—I slept like a stone dropped into deep water—so I gave her my commute from Tuesday, a hour spent between stations where the train lights flickered and I had almost understood something pivotal.  She opened the matchbox, inhaled the hour, and sneezed out a small blue feather.  “Close enough,” she said, and handed me the afternoons.

I carried 1998 in my pocket for weeks.  Whenever I reached in for change, I felt floppy-disk plastic, smelled sun-heated car seats, heard the first few bars of a song whose title would return to me only when I no longer needed it.  The year grew restless, pressed against the seam of my days, and one morning I woke to find my bedside clock blinking 00:00 over and over, a pupil who refuses to answer until called by the right name.

That night I used the question-mark key.  I pressed its curved head to the wall beside the window and the drywall sighed open like a book.  Inside was a corridor lined with coat hooks; from each dangled a version of me I had not become.  The first wore a lab coat, pockets full of glass eyes that winked independently.  The second wore river mud and carried a lantern made from a turtle shell.  The third wore nothing but the copper penny, enlarged to the size of a shield, held by leather straps.  They nodded politely, as one does to a cousin met at a funeral, and stepped aside so I could walk deeper.

At the end of the corridor was a room containing only a table and a jigsaw puzzle already started.  The pieces were not cardboard but moments: here a first kiss tasted of chlorine, here a hospital corridor smelled of burnt toast, here a lightning bug flickered Morse code against a mason jar.  I sat and began to fit the fragments together, but the picture on the box showed a mirror instead of an image; every piece I placed reflected a different angle of my face.  The more I completed, the older or younger I became, depending on which diagonal I followed.  I tried to stop when I reached the edge of twenty-nine and a half, but the puzzle had no border; it grew outward like frost on a windowpane.

A sound behind me: the landlady entered, no longer stooped but tall as a lamppost.  She carried the suitcase I had arrived with, now heavy again and rattling.  “You packed more than you knew,” she said, setting it down.  The latches sprang open.  Out floated every hour I had squandered: movies watched only to kill night, conversations rehearsed instead of spoken, the three-page diary begun on a January that never saw February.  The hours condensed into a small storm cloud overhead, crackling with unreleased minutes.

She handed me the original penny.  “Bite,” she instructed.  I did.  It tasted of railroad tracks and birthday candles.  When I looked at the bitten edge, I saw my teeth marks were actually letters spelling the town’s real name, the one too heavy for maps.  Speaking it aloud would anchor me forever or free me; the grammar remained ambiguous.

Instead I asked, “What happens to the unfinished things if no one claims them?”

“They finish themselves,” she said.  “Scarves knot into nests for paper cranes.  Novels grow final paragraphs from the last reader’s breath.  Bridges curve back into the iron hearts of stars.  Nothing stays incomplete; it only changes custody.”

I considered the cloud of wasted time above me.  It had begun to snow: each flake a minute landing on my shoulders, cold and weightless until accumulated.  I opened the matchbox that still contained the hour of almost-understanding from the train.  The woman had returned it during the night, slipped it under my door with a note: “I finished the dream; it ends with you waving back.”  I took the hour out, held it to the cloud, and watched the two recognize each other like twins separated at birth.  They merged into a soft gray rain that fell upward, returning to the ceiling, soaking into the puzzle.  The mirrored pieces dissolved, leaving only the table and the empty box.

The landlady smiled, young now, then old, then both at once.  “You’ve paid the deposit,” she said.  The question-mark key cooled against my chest, straightening into an exclamation.  I walked back down the corridor of unchosen selves; they applauded quietly, the sound of moths hitting screens.  The wall closed behind me.

Morning in Elsewhere tasted of tin and bread again.  My suitcase waited by the door, now packed with silence that weighed exactly what a heartbeat should.  I checked out; the landlady—stooped once more—returned the penny, unbitten, date shiny as promise.  “Spend it where time circulates,” she advised.  I left town the way children do: by unfolding the map, smoothing the crease with my palm, and letting the paper decide where next the world would open.

I have been twenty-nine and a half ever since, aging sideways, collecting unfinished things to return them to themselves.  Somewhere a scarf finishes its last row; somewhere a novel ends with the word “listen”; somewhere a bridge touches both banks at once and the river applauds.  I carry the penny in a pocket unstitched by years; it falls through holes, rolls ahead of me, always waiting heads-up on the sidewalk of whatever city I almost recognize.  I pick it up, bite gently, taste railroads and birthdays, and remember the town whose name I never spoke, the place that finishes what we leave undone—Elsewhere, the quiet partner to every story we abandon, patiently knitting the final period out of our loose, bright threads.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
